pages.title: FastEvents Overview
pages.purpose: High-level introduction to FastEvents: what it is, why it exists, its design philosophy, and how the major components relate. Links to child pages for deeper coverage.

pages.title: Getting Started
pages.purpose: Practical guide for new users: installation, Python version requirements, running the demo, and a minimal working example showing app + bus + handler.

pages.title: Architecture Overview
pages.purpose: Explains the three-layer architecture (Runtime/Bus, Application Core/Dispatcher+Injector, Top Layer/Handlers+Extensions), the Declaration vs Execution space separation, and the transport boundary concept.

pages.title: Core Concepts
pages.purpose: Overview of the fundamental building blocks of FastEvents: events, subscriptions, propagation levels, and the publish/consume lifecycle. Links to child pages for each concept.

pages.title: Event Model
pages.purpose: Detailed coverage of StandardEvent, RuntimeEvent, EventContext, and RuntimeEventView. Explains immutability, tag normalization, the new_event factory, and the BusPublisher protocol.

pages.title: Subscription and Tag Matching
pages.purpose: Explains the OR-of-ANDs subscription DSL, tag normalization rules, positive/negative patterns, glob matching via fnmatchcase, and the normalize_tags / normalize_subscription / matches_subscription functions.

pages.title: Level-Based Propagation
pages.purpose: Deep dive into the level system: negative levels for observation-only subscribers, level 0 for primary handlers, positive levels for fallback, concurrent same-level execution, and consumption semantics.

pages.title: Application API (FastEvents Class)
pages.purpose: Overview of the FastEvents declaration facade and its public API. Links to child pages covering handler registration, event streaming, and the extension mount surface.

pages.title: Registering Handlers with @app.on
pages.purpose: Covers the @app.on decorator: subscription input, level parameter, name parameter, HandlerSubscriber creation, and how handlers are added to the Dispatcher registry.

pages.title: Temporary Event Streams with app.listen
pages.purpose: Explains the app.listen async context manager, StreamSubscriber lifecycle, EventStream async iteration, cleanup guarantees, and use cases like testing and side-car monitoring.

pages.title: Publishing Events with app.publish
pages.purpose: Covers app.publish as the app-side transport boundary, the tags/payload/meta parameters, StandardEvent return value, BusNotStartedError guard, and the distinction from bus.publish.

pages.title: Runtime Bus
pages.purpose: Overview of the Bus abstraction and InMemoryBus implementation. Links to child pages covering lifecycle management and the internal dispatch pipeline.

pages.title: Bus Lifecycle Management
pages.purpose: Covers the Bus abstract interface, InMemoryBus state machine (_started/_accepting/_stopping flags), astart/astop/run/start/stop methods, drain-based shutdown, and BusAlreadyStartedError / BusNotStartedError guards.

pages.title: Event Dispatch Pipeline
pages.purpose: Explains the internal flow from bus.publish → asyncio.Queue → _dispatch_worker → ingest → dispatcher.dispatch, the RuntimePublishScope ContextVar for re-entrant publishing, and task tracking during shutdown.

pages.title: Dispatcher and Subscribers
pages.purpose: Overview of how the Dispatcher routes events to subscribers and how subscriber implementations work. Links to child pages for the Dispatcher and the subscriber/DI system.

pages.title: Dispatcher Internals
pages.purpose: Covers the Dispatcher class: subscriber registry, add/remove_subscriber, bind_runtime_publisher, the dispatch() algorithm (filter → group by level → concurrent gather → propagation control), ErrorHook, DispatcherSnapshot, and SubscriberSnapshot.

pages.title: Dependency Injection and Handler Execution
pages.purpose: Covers HandlerSubscriber, the _DependencyResolver pipeline (RuntimeEvent injection, Pydantic payload validation, @dependency resolution), DependencyScope caching, cycle detection via InjectionError, RecoverableInjectionFailure, and SessionNotConsumed flow control.

pages.title: RPC Extension
pages.purpose: Overview of the RpcExtension system that layers request-reply semantics over the async event bus. Links to child pages for the API and internal mechanics.

pages.title: RpcExtension API
pages.purpose: Covers RpcExtension.request_stream(), request_one(), and request() methods, RpcContext and rpc_context() dependency, RpcReplyStream async iterator, and the AppExtensions mount surface (app.ex).

pages.title: RPC Internals: Correlation and Reply Routing
pages.purpose: Explains the correlation ID mechanism (RPC_CORRELATION_ID_KEY / RPC_REPLY_TAGS_KEY), temporary listener setup via AsyncExitStack, metadata injection into request events, RpcContext.reply() routing, timeout logic in RpcReplyStream, and RpcRequestTimeoutError / RpcReplyNotAvailableError.

pages.title: Testing
pages.purpose: Guide to the test suite: what is covered, how to run tests, patterns used (bus lifecycle setup/teardown, DI caching tests, RPC round-trip tests), and the verify_rpc_di scripts.

pages.title: Unit and Integration Tests
pages.purpose: Covers tests/test_fastevents.py: test structure, bus lifecycle fixtures, tag normalization tests, level propagation tests, DI caching and cycle detection tests, payload validation fallback tests, and RPC extension tests.

pages.title: Demo and Verification Scripts
pages.purpose: Covers demo.py (streaming LLM chat demo with RpcExtension), verify_rpc_di.py scripts, and the deprecated examples in _deprecated/ showing the evolution of the API.

pages.title: Design Documents and Roadmap
pages.purpose: Overview of the RFC and development plan documents that govern the project's design decisions and future direction.

pages.title: RFC 0001: Core Model and Runtime Protocol
pages.purpose: Summarizes the formal RFC defining the v0 core: event model, subscription DSL, subscriber protocol, propagation rules, handler injection model, stream-style subscribers, dispatcher responsibilities, bus lifecycle, and request/reply protocol.

pages.title: Development Plan: Minimal Core Architecture
pages.purpose: Covers the development-plan.md: the 5-phase boundary-reduction plan, removal of request/reply from core Bus/EventContext, introduction of DI v1, formalization of app.ex extension mount surface, and design guardrails (no Bus in extensions, minimal EventContext, no heavy lifecycle).

pages.title: Glossary
pages.purpose: Definitions of all codebase-specific terms, jargon, abbreviations, and domain concepts that an onboarding engineer needs to know, with code pointers.
